<|det|>header [435, 23, 560, 37]<|/det|>CIVIL HISTORY.
<|det|>header [882, 23, 929, 37]<|/det|>1626.
<|det|>text [170, 46, 932, 160]<|/det|>since 1514, \( ^{1} \) and which had been applied to English merchant ships since 1553, was still untried in the Navy, possibly because it had been found to set up galvanic action with the iron of the rudder pintles, etc. Nor was Hawkmys's sheathing of double planks, with tar and hair between them, as generally adopted as it should have been, though it remained in some favour until late in the seventeenth century. Much more care, in fact, was devoted to making ships look well than to making them really serviceable; and the result often was that after her completion a vessel was found to be so crank that it was deemed necessary to "fur" or "girdle" her with a partial, or even an entire external planking beyond her original skin. \( ^{2} \)
<|det|>text [170, 160, 934, 380]<|/det|>As the Royal Prince \( ^{3} \) was the greatest constructive effort of the reign of James I., some account must be here given of her. Her crew was laid in Towndoch in October 26th, 1608, her chief constructor being Phineas Pett. Her nominal tonnage was 1200, but, measured according to the rules in force in 1632, it was 1035 nett, and 1330 gross. After the work had been some time in progress, and had met with much adverse criticism from rival shipwrights, a commission, consisting of Captain George Waymouth, Matthew Baker (who had been a principal constructor for more than half a century), William Bright, Edward Stevens, and others, was ordered to report on what was being done. Pett hated Waymouth, and Baker despised the Pett family, while Bright was particularly jealous of Phineas. But Pett had powerful protectors in the Lord High Admiral and Sir Robert Mansell; and after there had been not only inquiry and further inquiry, but also a special scrutiny by the King and Prince Henry, Phineas Pett emerged triumphant. An attempt to launch the ship was made on September 24th, 1610, but it failed, owing to the dockhead being too narrow to allow her to pass. She was, however, successfully launched a little later.
<|det|>text [172, 381, 934, 425]<|/det|>Pett, on this occasion, owed more to his protectors than to the merits of his work, for the Royal Prince, though a striking object in the water, was both ill-designed and ill-built. As early as 1621, it was reported to Buckingham \( ^{5} \) that although she had cost
<|det|>ref_text [236, 428, 911, 451]<|/det|>\( ^{1} \)  Duro'a Armada Español, 121.
 \( ^{2} \)  Harl. MSS. 2301: ‘Nomenclature Navalis’ (Manwaring's ‘Dictionary’).
 \( ^{3} \)  Often called Prince Royal, or simply Prince.
<|det|>ref_text [236, 451, 650, 462]<|/det|>\( ^{4} \)  Oppenheim, ‘Admin., of Royal Navy’, 204.
<|det|>ref_text [236, 462, 421, 472]<|/det|>\( ^{5} \)  Coke MSS. i. 114.
<|det|>list [236, 431, 911, 472]<|/det|>
<PAGE><|det|>page_number [50, 24, 81, 38]<|/det|>1625.]
<|det|>header [225, 24, 704, 38]<|/det|>THE WITHDRAWAL FROM CADIZ.
<|det|>page_number [788, 24, 819, 37]<|/det|>163
<|det|>text [45, 46, 882, 109]<|/det|>he found that the merchantmen had kept out of danger, and that the Dutch had lost two ships. When such conduct passed unpunished, it was easy to foresee what support could be counted on. Winnebikom gave further orders; the troops landed: Sir John Bright summoned gave forth orders; the troops landed: Sir John Bright summoned gave forth orders; the troops landed: Sir John Bright summoned gave forth orders; the troops landed: Sir John Bright summoned give forth orders; the troops landed: Sir John Bright summoned gave forth orders; the troops landed: Sir John Bright summoned gave forth orders; the troops landed: Sir John Bright
<|det|>text [45, 56, 882, 112]<|/det|>he found that men had been made to pursue the ships up the harbour. Winckeldon had been told that there was not enough water, and had asked no further. He told not consider that Drake had sailed into the harbour before him, and that he might have passed up, as Drake had passed, with the lead going on both sides. Throughout he ignored his commission. Instead of burning the Spanish ships, fifty-nine sail in all, as he had been ordered, he landed troops and marched towards Brazil's bridge. Arrived there he found that his troops had no food—no meat had been landed—and he stupidly moaned them into the village where was stored the wine for the West India fleets. The whole army at once drank itself into a state of madness, and all that Wimbledon could do was to decide that the bridge was not worth holding, or even destroying, and to march his troops back again. Yet this bridge was in 1596, had decided to build the bay on one way who would hold the town, for it was the only means of access from the mainland to Leon Island, on which Cádiz lies. All that Wimbledon did was, to take Fort Puntal and evacuate it, to take the bridge and abandon it, and to capture a few boats from St. Mary Pass, or even replace the long boats which had been lost on the rough passage out.
<|det|>text [45, 533, 782, 640]<|/det|>It was now decided to look for the Plate fleet, and, on November 4th, Wimbledon's squadrons took up their positions in a long line stretching south from Cape St. Vincent. They might have saved themselves the trouble and they known that the fleet had taken the southern route and, escaping up in shore, had reached Cadiz two days after the English had left it.
<|det|>text [45, 640, 782, 704]<|/det|>Wimbledon had intended to cruise till the 20th, but on the 16th he was persuaded to bear up for England. His ships were fast and leaking, the crews were dying fast. Burchett's tells a story that,
<|det|>list [200, 712, 625, 761]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_footnote [200, 712, 365, 725]<|/det|>* 'Travers' p. 275;
<|det|>page_footnote [201, 725, 625, 737]<|/det|>* Called the bridge of Savez, in vol. I, 341, 345.
<|det|>page_footnote [201, 737, 353, 749]<|/det|>* *Eustis' p. 275.
<|det|>page_footnote [201, 749, 425, 761]<|/det|>* Bk. III. Chap. xvii.
<PAGE><|det|>page_number [165, 22, 194, 36]<|/det|>68
<|det|>header [366, 22, 732, 37]<|/det|>MILITARY HISTORY, 1603-1649.
<|det|>header [879, 22, 933, 37]<|/det|>[1627.
<|det|>text [160, 45, 938, 92]<|/det|>sent home to hasten the Irish regiments and the supplies which had been promised. There was also a report abroad \( ^{1} \) that a fleet of forty Spanish warships and twenty Dunquerqueurs would soon arrive to help the French in holding the island. \( ^{2} \)
<|det|>text [160, 92, 936, 253]<|/det|>For two days Buckingham busied himself in landing guns, but in his determination to reduce St. Martin's, he failed to see that trenches would be needed. It was not until August that these were made. In the interval, provisions began to reach the fort. The ships could not keep out the small boats used for this service. No small craft had been provided with the expedition, and even of the ships' boats many had been lost. An attack on repeated the ships was beaten off on the 23rd, and was not repeated. The boats of the fleet rowed guard to keep provisions out of the fort, but with indifferent success. The siege began to seem tedious to Buckingham, and, against advice, an assault was delivered.
<|det|>text [160, 253, 936, 331]<|/det|>When this was repulsed, he decided that the siege must be laid in form, and, on August 20th, he began a boom which should keep provisions from the citadel. A storm destroyed this before it was complete: it was therefore replaced by bavsers stretched from ship to ship.
<|det|>text [160, 331, 936, 440]<|/det|>Meanwhile, on 20d September, some reinforcements, two thousand five hundred strong, arrived under Sir Ralph Bingley. Holland was still expected with eight thousand. In his great need of men, Buckingham had landed five hundred sailors under Captain Weddell, but they proved of little use. Bingley's Irish troops were ordered to assault La Pré. A few guns were landed at La Flotte, but, when the preparations were well advanced, the order was foolishly cancelled.
<|det|>text [160, 440, 936, 530]<|/det|>Provisions were running short. They had been ordered in England, \( ^{2} \) but bad weather had delayed their departure. When Sir John Burgh was killed, even Buckingham began to despair of success. Though the Rochester had joined us there was little help to be had from them. Even such provisions as could be procured over the bayous they cancelled. Even such transport was called for September, Sir Henry Palmer arrived from England with the
<|det|>text [194, 532, 936, 545]<|/det|>\( ^{1} \)  “Hazl. Misc,” v. 111.
<|det|>text [164, 545, 934, 557]<|/det|>\( ^{2} \)  “Hazl. text” between France and Spain had been signless on religious grounds.
<|det|>text [164, 557, 671, 568]<|/det|>Politically, there could be no union between the countries.
<|det|>text [194, 568, 936, 580]<|/det|>\( ^{3} \)  Cal. N. P. Dom. lxxx. 45, and cf. *Feuerle, VIII. ii. 207.
<|det|>text [195, 580, 363, 591]<|/det|>\( ^{4} \)  “Hazl. Misc.” v. 3.

<|det|>title [460, 111, 671, 126]<|/det|>CHAPTER XIX.
<|det|>text [336, 133, 792, 147]<|/det|>VOYAGES AND DISCOVERIES, 1608–1649.
<|det|>text [306, 155, 812, 169]<|/det|>SIR CLEMENTS MARKHAM, K.C.B., F.R.S.
<|det|>text [180, 177, 948, 237]<|/det|>Prince Henry supports discovery—The early ventures of the East India Company—Saris to Japan—Attempts at the North-West Passage—George Waymouth—John Knight—The Massey Company and the North-East Passage—George Waymouth—The three voyages and death—Prince Henry and Ralph—Thomas Burton—Thomas and the ships was beaten off on the 23rd, and was not repeated. The boats of the fleet rowed guard to keep provisions out of the fort, but with indifference success. The siege began to seem tedious to Buckingham, and, against advice, an assault was delivered.
<|det|>text [179, 238, 948, 300]<|/det|>When this was repulsed, he decided that the siege must be laid in form, and, on August 20th, he began a boom which should keep provision from the citadel. A storm destroyed this before it was complete: it was therefore replaced by brawsers stretched from ship to ship.
<|det|>text [179, 300, 948, 395]<|/det|>Meanwhile, on 2nd September, some reinforcements, two thousand five hundred strong, arrived under Sir Ralph Bingley. Holland was still expected with eight thousand. In his great need of men, Buckingham landed five hundred soldiers under Captain Weddell, but they proved of little use. Bingley's Irish troops were ordered to assault La Prée. A few guns were landed at La Flotte, but, when the preparations were well advanced, the order was foolishly cancelled.
<|det|>text [179, 395, 948, 509]<|/det|>Provisions were running short. They had been ordered in England, \( ^{2} \) but bad weather had delayed their departure. When Sir John Burgh was kilted, even Buckingham began to despair of success. Though the Rochester had joined its forces there was little help to him. Though then, Even such provision as could be procured for them were only bought at an exorbitant rate. \( ^{4} \) But at the end of September, Sir Henry Palmer arrived from England with the
<|det|>text [180, 510, 948, 539]<|/det|>\( ^{1} \)  “Had, Mie,” v. 111.
 \( ^{2} \)  The treaty between France and Spain had been on religious grounds.
Politically, there could be no union between the countries.
<|det|>text [180, 539, 705, 551]<|/det|>\( ^{3} \)  Cal. N. P. Dom. lxxx. 45, and cf. *Feuerle, VIII. ii.207.
<|det|>text [180, 551, 363, 563]<|/det|>\( ^{4} \)  ‘Harl. Miee’s, v. 3.

<PAGE><|det|>page_number [187, 28, 216, 42]<|/det|>84
<|det|>header [344, 28, 830, 42]<|/det|>VOYAGES AND DISCOVERIES, 1603–1649.
<|det|>header [904, 28, 955, 42]<|/det|>[1610.
<|det|>text [183, 51, 958, 206]<|/det|>selected to lead this daring enterprise. He fitted out a little vessel of eighty tons, and weighed anchor at Greenwich on May 1st, 1607. He had with him only ten men, and his own little son John. Hudson first sighted the east coast of Greenland in 73° N. on the 22nd of June, and then examined the edge of the ice until he sighted the N.W. cape of Spitzbergen, and named it Hakluyt Headland. He made a careful examination of the western coast of Spitzbergen, and on his way home discovered the island since called Jan Mayen, which he named "Hudson's Tutches."
<|det|>text [183, 207, 961, 560]<|/det|>In 1608 Hudson fitted out a second expedition to explore the sea between Spitzbergen and Novaya Zeniya. He coasted along the edge of the ice, making more than one resolute effort to penetrate Hudson's waters and that the provisions would not last long. All this, the previously结论 of the previous work, did not lead to any new records to distinguish the number of mouths by turning the sick and weak adrift. As they knew that their captain would never consent to this crime, he and his son were also to be abandoned. After the ship had been three days at sea, the diabolical act was perpetuated. Four sick men, three who were loyal, Captain Hudson and his son Jack, who had accompanied him to all his voyages and who was then eighteen, were forced into the shallop, and she was cast adrift. They were given a fowling-piece, a little ammunition, an iron pot, and some meal. Hudson and his doomed companions were never heard of more. Eleven men remained on board. Only five survived to reach England, including the Boyd the mate, and Pickett, a servant of Sir Dudley Digges, who wrote the narrative. No one was punished, yet there was a feeling that search should be made for the great navigator. He had found a grave in the midst of his discoveries, and his name is immortalised by the strait and the inland sea which bear his name.
<|det|>text [84, 561, 812, 720]<|/det|>At that period the natural leaders of maritime enterprise were also more or less disabled. Sir Walter Raleigh, the accomplished navigator and fervent patriot, was in prison, having been condemned on a false charge. Prince Henry, on whom the mantle of his godmother had fallen, was burnt to a pile full of lead and odour, but inexperience. The friendship between the illustrious prisoner and the young Prince is a very touching episode in our history. Queen Anne first took her son to see Raleigh in 1606, when the boy was only twelve years old; but their most intimate friendship was from 1610 to the Prince's untimely death in 1612. Prince Henry constantly consulted Raleigh, and was much guided by his advice. He learned the rudiments of shipbuilding from Phineas
<PAGE><|det|>page_number [74, 20, 128, 35]<|/det|>1602]
<|det|>header [388, 20, 530, 34]<|/det|>NOVAL LAW.
<|det|>page_number [819, 20, 855, 34]<|/det|>106
<|det|>text [70, 40, 856, 707]<|/det|>on December 25th, 1652, a modification of these rules was enacted for the government of the whole Navy. These were the first regular Articles of War; and to Mr. Oppenheim belongs the credit of being the first to point out that interesting fact. Until he did so it had been generally supposed that the consecutive history of the Articles of War began in 1661; but, as Mr. Oppenheim justly says, “these latter were only based upon those previously existing, which are the groundwork of all subsequent modifications and additions experience has shown to be necessary down to the present day.” \( ^{9} \)  There were thirty-nine of them, thirteen prescribing death, and twelve more prescribing either death or a living pandemic almost eternally, according to the twenty made except. Three men of the Portland, convicted of that most serious offence, or of having incited to it, in 1653, when the country was at war, were let off with the comparatively light though very cruel penalty of having to stand for one hour with their right hands nailed to the mainstay of the flagstaff and with halters about their necks. Three of their fellows received only thirty lashes apiece.
<|det|>text [70, 707, 856, 802]<|/det|>No force akin to the modern Royal Marines was afloat in British ships until after the Restoration; but the urgency of the situation during the Dutch War led to the embarkation of considerable bodies of soldiers, who assisted in the work of the vessels, and, in fact, did marines' duties. They behaved extremely well, although they went on board under none of their own officers above the rank of sergeant; and, no doubt, it was the recollection of their excellent service that suggested the formation, in the days of Charles II., of a regular force of sea soldiers.
<|det|>text [71, 802, 854, 833]<|/det|>There was still no uniform for naval officers, and seamen dressed as they chose. The practice, prevalent in the early part of the reign
<|det|>page_footnote [70, 839, 856, 902]<|/det|>\( ^{1} \)  The rules of 1652 and 1661, however, must not be regarded as the sole source from which the present Articles of War have been evolved. Additional sources continued to be supplied, by means of successive Admirals' instructions, until the commencement of the eighteen century; and only then did it cease to be the practice for Commanders-in-Chief to issue, as it were, special laws of their own. For a further discussion of this subject, see Chap. XXVI.
<|det|>page_footnote [101, 900, 621, 914]<|/det|>\( ^{2} \)  ‘Admin. of Roy. Navy’, 311.—‘Thomason Pamphlets’.”
